text: um i want you to step into a very specific point in history with us for a minute right it is a
pretty intense moment to think about it really is picture the absolute terror of surviving the fall
of your own family yeah imagine your grandfather was a king and your father was the beloved prince
your whole entire world is built on this totally solid foundation of royal power exactly you have
everything and then in just one single afternoon on a battlefield far away that entire foundation
just crumbles it is gone your grandfather and your father are killed the enemy armies are coming fast
your family's rule is over in just a matter hours and you are only five years old when the scary
news reaches the palace right you were just a little kid yeah and in the total panic to get out
of there yeah your nurse picks you up to run but she is running so fast in the chaos that she trips
she drops you she drops you you fall and the injuries are just terrible yeah they are permanent
you were left permanently crippled in both of your feet and from that day forward your life becomes
this dark shadowy existence just hiding hiding living completely off the grid in a dusty remote
little town you wake up every single morning expecting the new king's assassins to finally
kick down your door the psychological weight of that situation is just staggering especially when
you consider how
things worked back then the political rules of the ancient middle east exactly what we are describing
isn't just a dramatic story it was the standard rule for changing governments it was what everybody
did yeah whenever a new family took power the absolute first order of business was completely
wiping out the old royal family every single one of them everyone leaving a rival alive was basically
political suicide even a little kid even a surviving grandson who was crippled he was still
a symbol that a rebellion could rise and fall but he was still a symbol that a rebellion could rise and fall
he was still a symbol that a rebellion could rise and fall but he was still a symbol that a rebellion could rise and fall
around wow so hiding in total fear wasn't him overreacting it was really the only logical
thing to do which is exactly what makes the events we were talking about today so totally shocking
it breaks all the rules it really does today we are doing our deep dive exclusively into chapter
9 of 2 samuel we are just going to look at this one single chapter we are going to ignore the rest
of the book and just focus right here on these specific events exactly we are not doing a big
fast overview we are slowing time way down down to a crawl we want to examine this one brief meeting
and to make it really clear we are using the bible in basic english translation today it keeps the
language very simple and easy to understand it does and to truly understand how huge these 13
verses are we are using some amazing sources we have the historical details from the logos
adventist bible commentary right and the deep psychological insights from lng white's commentary
plus the really brilliant literary analysis from robert alter from his book the art of biblical
narrative we are going to look at the psychology and the tense politics and the amazing way this
story is written because focusing exclusively on this one chapter lets us see all the tiny
brilliant details people usually just read right past it they do they rush past the talking to get
to the next big battle but when you hit the brakes and look at the actual words they say to each
other yeah and the specific towns they mention you find this master class in human psychology
this chapter completely changes the rules of power it introduces a kind of unconditional grace
that shocks everybody from the rich people in the palace to the terrified guy hiding in the desert
everybody is surprised so let's get into the first verse it sets a very specific stage david is the
king now and he is totally safe yes he has survived years of running for his life he won the
civil wars he secured his borders against his enemies his capital city in jerusalem is strong
ultimate security and power and um usually when a leader gets total power their true character
comes out the mask comes off exactly without enemies forcing them to be careful they show
who they really are and for a lot of rulers ultimate safety makes them extremely paranoid
oh yeah history is full of that the assyrians the babylonians the early roman kings
total power made them ruthless they start hunting down anyone who might maybe be a threat 10 years
later but david's first big action in this time of total safety goes in the completely opposite
direction he must have confused his generals so much i mean he calls his advisors together
not to order a mass killing but to ask a question the text says the king asks is there any one of
saul's family still living to whom i may be a friend in god's name think about how crazy that
question is politically saul was the old king who spent years using the whole army to hunt david
down like an animal saul hated him yes saul's family was the old government to actively look
for survivors of that family is just inviting danger into a safe kingdom his generals were
probably standing there thinking um why are we bringing up the past we finally have peace
right but david is not thinking about politics here he uses a very specific deep
phrase he wants to be a friend in god's name the logos commentary is so helpful here it
gives us a really important anchor for that phrase it really does in the original language
this is tied to a word that the commentary especially the portuguese edition of the logos
commentary translates beautifully what does it call it it calls it bondad de dos which means
the kindness of god the kindness of god wow right because this is not normal human kindness human
kindness is almost always a trade i do something for you and you eventually do something for me
it is networking it is building up favors exactly but the kindness of god operates on
a totally different level it is unconditional it
flows from the person who has everything down to the person who has nothing with absolutely zero
expectation of getting paid back none at all david is choosing to act like god acts toward broken
people that idea of acting like god is so deep especially when you remember the very personal
history behind all this the promise he made to jonathan yes ellen g white's writings talk a lot
about this unique friendship jonathan was saul's oldest son he was the next in line for the throne
he was the first king of the kingdom he was the first king of the kingdom he was the first king of
